result:
Two main strategies.

Chaining: each bucket holds linked list (or tree). Collisions append to list. Lookup walks chain comparing keys. Java HashMap uses this; switches to red-black tree when chain gets long.

Open addressing: probe for next empty slot in array itself. Linear probing walks +1, +2, +3. Quadratic probing uses +1, +4, +9. Double hashing uses second hash function for step size. Python dict, Go map use variants of this.

Tradeoffs: chaining tolerates high load factor, simpler delete, but pointer-chasing hurts cache. Open addressing stays cache-friendly, no per-entry allocation, but needs resize earlier (load factor ~0.7) and delete needs tombstones.

Both rely on good hash function to spread keys. Bad hash collapses everything into one bucket, turns O(1) into O(n).
